did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep learning-based approach employing the YOLOv8 network for melanoma detection and fine-tuning models for segmentation of melanoma in dermoscopic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as 'TYPE Original Research' and presents a new diagnostic aid tool called the Health of Things Melanoma Detection System (HTMDS). It details original experimental methodology using YOLOv8 networks for detection and segmentation, reports specific quantitative results (e.g., >98% accuracy for detection, >99% for segmentation), and compares these new findings against state-of-the-art methods, which characterizes it as an original experimental study rather than a review.

title: Health of Things Melanoma Detection System—detection and segmentation of melanoma in dermoscopic images applied to edge computing using deep learning and fine-tuning models
brief_summary: This study proposes the Health of Things Melanoma Detection System (HTMDS), a deep learning-based diagnostic aid for detecting and segmenting melanoma in dermoscopic images using edge computing. The system utilizes the YOLOv8 framework for melanoma detection and employs fine-tuning models combined with techniques like region growing and Parzen windows for precise segmentation. The methodology involves a workflow where mobile devices capture images via a dermatoscope and upload them to a cloud API for processing. The proposed approach achieved over 98% accuracy for detection and over 99% accuracy for segmentation, with the YOLOv8x model specifically reaching 98.67% accuracy. These results surpass various state-of-the-art manual, semi-automatic, and automatic methods, demonstrating the system's effectiveness for real-time medical diagnosis in smart city applications.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses exclusively on the detection and segmentation of melanoma in dermoscopic images. The system (HTMDS) is designed to identify melanoma regions and segment the lesion surface. The paper mentions differentiating between benign and malignant melanomas in the context of general CAD systems but specifies that the proposed method targets melanoma detection and segmentation.

all_task_types.details: The paper uses the YOLOv8 framework for melanoma detection, generating bounding boxes and binary detection masks to identify the presence and location of melanoma in dermoscopic images. The system outputs a confirmation of the presence of melanoma (binary classification/detection) and the bounding box coordinates.

The paper employs fine-tuning models combined with techniques like region growing, Parzen windows, and K-means clustering to segment the melanoma lesion surface. The output includes a binary mask segmentation image representing the precise pixels belonging to the melanoma region.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A database of dermatoscopic images originated from microscopic skin examinations magnified 20 times, from the Dermatology Service of Hospital Pedro Hispano in Portugal. Images have a resolution of 8 bits per pixel and dimensions of 768 × 560 pixels.

use.training: Not explicitly reported as training data for the proposed HTMDS; mentioned in the context of other studies (e.g., Ünver and Ayan, 2019) using it for validation and comparison.
use.validation: Mentioned in the literature review as used for validation and comparison with state-of-the-art methods by other researchers.
use.testing: Not explicitly reported as testing data for the proposed HTMDS.
use.other: Referenced in the introduction/literature review as a challenging dataset used in prior work.

datasets_used.name: ISBI 2017 database
datasets_used.description: A dataset used in prior studies for skin lesion segmentation and validation.

use.training: Not explicitly reported as training data for the proposed HTMDS.
use.validation: Mentioned in the literature review as used for validation and comparison with state-of-the-art methods by other researchers (e.g., Ünver and Ayan, 2019).
use.testing: Not explicitly reported as testing data for the proposed HTMDS.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2021
datasets_used.description: A dataset mentioned in the context of a review of research spanning 2018 to 2021.

use.training: Not explicitly reported as training data for the proposed HTMDS.
use.other: Referenced in the introduction/literature review as part of a catalog of publications.

datasets_used.name: Custom Dataset (Unspecified)
datasets_used.description: The dataset used to train and evaluate the proposed Health of Things Melanoma Detection System (HTMDS). The paper states that the models were trained using 'the data set presented in Section 3.1', but Section 3.1 only describes the PH2 database in the context of other studies. The specific source, size, and split of the dataset used for the HTMDS experiments are not explicitly detailed in the provided text chunks.

use.training: Used to train various versions of YOLOv8 (n, s, m, l, x) for melanoma detection.
use.testing: Used to evaluate detection accuracy (e.g., YOLOv8x achieved 98.67% ACC) and segmentation performance.
use.other: Used for fine-tuning segmentation models (Parzen windowing, region growing, K-means).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To express the degree of dispersion and uniformity of the detection results across different YOLOv8 models and fine-tuning methods.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Statistical analysis was limited to descriptive statistics, specifically reporting mean values and standard deviations for performance metrics (Accuracy, Dice coefficient, Sensitivity, Specificity, etc.) across different YOLOv8 model versions and segmentation methods.

Custom Dataset (Unspecified).notes: The paper reports that the proposed method achieved over 98% accuracy for detection and over 99% accuracy for segmentation. YOLOv8x was identified as the best model for detection with the lowest standard deviation. The best segmentation model outperformed state-of-the-art methods in all reported metrics.

architecture_used.main_model: YOLOv8 (versions n, s, m, l, x)
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; weights were generated and stored in the API after the training process.

architecture_details.backbone_structure: Structure based on R-CNN, utilizing deeper layers to generate deep attributes for region proposals.
architecture_details.mechanism: Generates region proposals using deep layers, performs multiple proposal overlays to identify the region of interest, concatenates ROIs via ROI Poller to form the ideal detection bounding box, and constructs a binary detection mask classifying internal pixels.

training_details.notes: Training was conducted using a custom dataset. The YOLOv8x model was identified as having a more robust architecture, longer training time, and typically a higher number of training epochs compared to other versions.

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Bounding box coordinates and binary detection mask.
classification_or_output_head.post_processing: Morphological operation of erosion on the binary detection mask to smooth edges, followed by fine-tuning using Parzen window, K-means clustering, and region growing techniques.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Deep convolutional network pixel wise segmentation (Badrinarayanan et al., 2017)
Full resolution convolution networks (Al-Masni et al., 2018)
Deep class-specific learning features segmentation (Bi et al., 2019)
FLog Parzen level set (de Souza Rebouças et al., 2021)
preprocessing_techniques: Image transmission in Base64 format to reduce file size and minimize transmission risks
Morphological operation of erosion on the binary detection mask to smooth edges
conclusions: The proposed Health of Things Melanoma Detection System (HTMDS) effectively integrates deep learning and fine-tuning models for melanoma detection and segmentation in dermoscopic images applied to edge computing.
The system achieved over 98% accuracy for melanoma detection and over 99% accuracy for skin cancer segmentation, surpassing various state-of-the-art manual, semi-automatic, and automatic methods.

Among the YOLOv8 variants tested, the YOLOv8x model demonstrated the best performance for melanoma detection, achieving 98.67% accuracy with the lowest standard deviation, indicating high uniformity and reliability.

The YOLOv8x model outperformed other YOLOv8 versions in key metrics including Specificity (SPE > 99%), Positive Predictive Value (PPV), Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC), and Jaccard Index (JAC).

The best proposed segmentation model achieved an accuracy of 98.68%, Jaccard Index of 90.02%, and Dice coefficient of 94.66%, outperforming state-of-the-art methods such as SDA, U-Net, and various clustering and contour-based techniques.

The integration of edge computing resulted in significant gains in response time, with all detection models achieving inference times of less than 1 second, making them suitable for real-time cloud processing and smart city applications.

The system enables real-time, automatic detection and precise localization of melanoma regions without human intervention, providing diagnostic aids such as bounding boxes, segmentation masks, and detection reliability metrics to medical professionals.

The study highlights the potential of the HTMDS for population use and medical diagnosis in smart cities, although it acknowledges that the classification of specific melanoma types requires further study and labeled databases.
important_remarks: The specific source, size, and class distribution of the custom dataset used for training and evaluating the HTMDS are not explicitly reported, creating ambiguity regarding data provenance and potential selection bias.

The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to validate the significance of performance improvements over state-of-the-art methods; statistical analysis is limited to descriptive statistics (mean and standard deviation).

External validation on standard public datasets (e.g., PH2, ISIC 2021) is not performed for the proposed HTMDS; these datasets are only referenced in the literature review to contextualize prior work.
The study focuses exclusively on melanoma detection and segmentation, explicitly noting that the classification of specific melanoma subtypes requires further study and labeled databases.

Performance metrics for the proposed method are compared against state-of-the-art methods from literature, but direct head-to-head comparison on the same test set is not established, limiting the validity of the comparative claims.

The paper acknowledges that the system's ability to maintain accuracy in variable clinical scenarios and adverse imaging conditions requires further examination, particularly regarding robustness in real-world deployment.
